judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 1 fully covers all five expectations (including explicitly tying the ROTA-214 commit to all three config files and adding a boot-time role-privilege check), while Report 2 misses the multi-file commit attribution and omits any startup/boot-time enforcement check, relying only on CI/lint/review gates.

report_1.evidence: the table owner bypasses row-level security policies by default, regardless of the BYPASSRLS role attribute, unless the table has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY set — and 0003_rls.sql never sets FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY on appointments. The application's runtime DATABASE_URL in .env.example:1, docker-compose.yml:5, and infra/main.tf:2 all point at juniper_owner

report_2.evidence: Postgres row-level security policies do not apply to the table owner unless FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY is also set — which it never is anywhere in db/migrations/. juniper_owner is a normal LOGIN role, not a superuser and not BYPASSRLS... it's exempt purely because it's the object's owner... docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1, and infra/main.tf:2-3 all configure the running API's DATABASE_URL to connect as juniper_owner

report_1.evidence:
Introduced in commit b2a23d2 ("ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot", 2026-06-17). Diff:
```
- DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_app:apppw@db:5432/juniper
+ DATABASE_URL=postgresql://juniper_owner:ownerpw@db:5432/juniper
```
applied identically to .env.example, docker-compose.yml, and infra/main.tf.

Revert the runtime DATABASE_URL in .env.example:1, docker-compose.yml:5, and infra/main.tf:2 back to juniper_app (least-privilege, non-owner, no BYPASSRLS)... Add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; in a new migration

report_2.evidence: revert the runtime API's DATABASE_URL to use juniper_app (apppw) for request-serving, and run the on-boot migration step as a separate one-off step/container using juniper_owner... add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_1.evidence: Add an application-level tenant filter (WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id, using the same claims["tenant_id"] already extracted in backend/app/deps.py) to backend/app/routes/appointments.py:16-19, per project documentation's defense-in-depth guidance — isolation should not depend on exactly one mechanism.

report_2.evidence: Defense-in-depth at the query layer: add an explicit WHERE tenant_id = :tid in backend/app/routes/appointments.py's query, so isolation doesn't rely on a single mechanism (RLS) with no redundancy.

report_1.evidence: Add a boot-time check (e.g. in backend/app/db.py or app startup) that queries SELECT rolbypassrls FROM pg_roles WHERE rolname = current_user and... refuses to start (or alerts loudly) if the connected role can bypass RLS on tenant tables.
